standard_description: Use special triangles to determine geometrically the values of sine, cosine, tangent for π/3, π/4 and π/6, and use the unit circle to express the values of sine, cosines, and tangent for π–x, π+x, and 2π–x in terms of their values for x, where x is any real number.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Given:
$${{f(x)}=\mathrm{cos}(2x)}$$
$${g(x)=-\mathrm{cos}(x)}$$
###IMAGE0###
a.   Describe the period, amplitude, and midline of the function
$${f(x)}$$
.
b.   Where is
$${f(x)}=g(x)$$
over the domain of
$${0\leq x \leq 2\pi}$$
?

Problem 1
Find
$${\mathrm{sin}(u-v)}$$
given
$${\mathrm{sin}(u)={8\over17}}$$
with
$${0\leq u \leq {\pi\over2}}$$
and
$${\mathrm{cos}(v)=-{24\over25}}$$
with
$${\pi \leq v \leq {3\pi\over2}}$$
.
Problem 2
Evaluate:
$${\mathrm{sin}(-15^{\circ})}$$
$${\mathrm{cos}(15^{\circ})}$$

A Ferris wheel is 50 meters in diameter and rotates once every three minutes. The center axle of the Ferris wheel is 30 meters from the ground.
a. Using the axes below, sketch a graph to show how the height of a passenger will vary with time. Assume that when the wheel starts rotating when the passenger is at the bottom.
###IMAGE0###
b. A mathematical model for this motion is given by the formula
$${h=a+b\mathrm{cos}ct}$$
where
$$h$$
= the height of the car in meters
$$t$$
= the time that has elapsed in minutes
$${a, b, c}$$
are constants
Find values for
$$a$$
,
$$b$$
, and
$$c$$
that will model this situation.

Problem 1
Prove the cofunction identity of:
$${\mathrm{cos}\left({\pi\over2}-x\right)=\mathrm{sin}x}$$
Using the sum or difference formula.
Problem 2
Solve:
$${\mathrm{sin}\left(x+{\pi\over4}\right)+1=\mathrm{sin}\left({\pi\over4}-x\right)}$$
for
$${0 \leq x \leq 2\pi}$$
.

Draw and label a figure on the circle that illustrates the relationship of the trigonometric tangent functions
$${\mathrm{tan}=\frac{\mathrm{sin}(\theta^\circ)}{\mathrm{cos}(\theta^\circ)}}$$
and the geometric tangent line to a circle through the point
$${(1,0)}$$
when
$${\theta=60}$$
. Explain the relationship, labeling the figure as needed.
###IMAGE0###

Problem 1
The points on the graphs and the unit circle below were chosen so that there is a relationship between them.
Explain the relationship between the coordinates
$$a$$
,
$$b$$
,
$$c$$
, and
$$d$$
marked on the graph of
$$y=\sin(t) $$
and
$$y=\cos(t)$$
and the quantities
$$A$$
,
$$B$$
, and
$$C$$
marked in the diagram of the unit circle below.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Of sine and cosine, one function is even and the other is odd. Why is this the case?
Problem 3
Match each graph with its equation.
###IMAGE1###
$${y=\mathrm{sin}x}$$
$${y=2\mathrm{sin}x}$$
$${y=\mathrm{sin}(2x)}$$
$${y=\mathrm{sin}x}+2$$

Task
Use the unit circle and indicated triangle below to find the exact value of the sine and cosine of the special angle $\pi/6.$
###IMAGE0###

Problem 1
If
$${{{{{360}}}}}$$
degrees is
$${2{\pi}}$$
radians, how many degrees are
$${\pi}$$
radians?
If
$${{{{{360}}}}}$$
degrees is
$${2{\pi}}$$
radians, how many degrees are
$$\frac{{\pi}}{3}$$
radians?
If
$${{{{{360}}}}}$$
degrees is
$${2{\pi}}$$
radians, how many radians are
$${90}$$
degrees?
If
$${{{{{360}}}}}$$
degrees is
$${2{\pi}}$$
radians, how many radians are
$${540}$$
degrees?
If
$${{{{{360}}}}}$$
degrees is
$${2{\pi}}$$
radians, how many degrees are
$$-\frac{5{\pi}}{4}$$
radians?
Problem 2
How many radii does it take to go around the whole circle? How would you represent this in terms of pi?
###IMAGE0###
Problem 3
Use the unit circle and indicated triangle below to find the exact value of the sine and cosine of the special angle
$${\frac{\pi}{4}}$$
.
###IMAGE1###

Problem 1
Are these expressions equivalent? Why or why not?
a.
$${\mathrm{sin}(a+b)}$$
b.
$${\mathrm{sin}a+\mathrm{sin}b}$$
How can you justify your reasoning when
$${a={\pi\over6}}$$
and
$${b={\pi\over3}}$$
? Use the unit circle shown below to justify your thinking.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Below are the sum and difference formulas for sine, cosine, and tangent.
$${\mathrm{sin}(a+b)=\mathrm{sin}a\mathrm{cos}b+\mathrm{cos}a\mathrm{sin}b}$$
$${\mathrm{sin}(a-b)=\mathrm{sin}a\mathrm{cos}b-\mathrm{cos}a\mathrm{sin}b}$$
$${\mathrm{cos}(a+b)=\mathrm{cos}a\mathrm{cos}b-\mathrm{sin}a\mathrm{sin}b}$$
$${\mathrm{cos}(a-b)=\mathrm{cos}a\mathrm{cos}b+\mathrm{sin}a\mathrm{sin}b}$$
$${\mathrm{tan}(a+b)={\mathrm{tan}a+\mathrm{tan}b\over{1-\mathrm{tan}a\mathrm{tan}b}}}$$
$${\mathrm{tan}(a-b)={\mathrm{tan}a-\mathrm{tan}b\over{1+\mathrm{tan}a\mathrm{tan}b}}}$$
Use both sum and difference formulas to find the value of:
$${\mathrm{sin}(75^{\circ})}$$
$${\mathrm{tan}(15^{\circ})}$$
$${\mathrm{cos}(105^{\circ})}$$
Problem 3
Find
$${\mathrm{cos}(a+b)}$$
given that
$${\mathrm{cos}(a)=-{4\over5}}$$
with
$${\pi \leq a \leq {3\pi\over2}}$$
and
$${\mathrm{sin}(b)={5\over13}}$$
with
$${0 \leq b \leq {\pi\over2}}$$
.

Problem 1
We did these problems a few lessons ago when we were solving for angles in right triangles.
$${\mathrm{sin}x={1\over2}}$$
$${\mathrm{cos}^{-1}\left(\sqrt2\over2\right)=x}$$
Which has infinite solutions and which has only one solution? Describe your reasoning.
Problem 2
Find all the solutions within the domain of
$${0 \leq x \leq 2\pi}$$
for the following equation.
$${2\mathrm{cos}(x)-1=0}$$

Problem 1
Solve for the general solution:
$${3\mathrm{tan}^2x-3=0}$$
$${2\mathrm{cos}^2(x)-\sqrt3 \mathrm{cos}x=0}$$
$${2\mathrm{cos}^2x+\mathrm{cos}x-1=0}$$
Problem 2
Solve for the domain
$${0 \leq \theta \leq 2\pi}$$
:
$${2\mathrm{sin}^2\theta+\mathrm{cos}\theta\mathrm{sin}^2\theta=0}$$

Write a general solution for:
a.
$${2\mathrm{cos}(2x)-\sqrt3=0}$$
b.
$${-\mathrm{tan}(3x)=1}$$

Problem 1
Below are two trigonometric equations and the graphs of the left-hand side of each equation.
###TABLE0###
How are the equations and the graphs similar? Different?
Problem 2
Find all of the solutions, algebraically, within the domain
$${0\leq x \leq 2\pi}$$
.
$${2\mathrm{sin}(2x)+2=3}$$
###IMAGE0###
Problem 3
Solve:
$${-3\mathrm{tan}\left({\pi\over2}x\right)=\sqrt3}$$

Problem 1
Convert
$${60^\circ}$$
to radians.
Problem 2
Convert
$${-\frac{\pi}{2}}$$
rad to degrees.
Problem 3
Evaluate
$${\mathrm{cos}\frac{3\pi}{4}}$$
.
Problem 4
If you evaluated
$${\mathrm{sin}3.1}$$
on your calculator in radian mode, would the answer be positive or negative? Why?

Problem 1
Evaluate the following trigonometric expressions and explain how you used the unit circle to determine your answer.
a.
$${\mathrm{sin}\left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}$$
b.
$${\mathrm{cos}\left(2\pi-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)}$$
Problem 2
Corinne says that for any real number
$${\theta}$$
,
$$\mathrm{cos}{\theta}=\mathrm{cos}{\theta}-\pi$$
. Is she correct? Explain how you know.

Find the general solution for:
$${\mathrm{cos}\left({\pi\over2}+x\right)=\mathrm{tan}{\pi\over4}}$$

Have students participate in the Desmos activity
Burning Daylight
.

Solve the equation for
$${0 \leq x \leq 2\pi}$$
.
$${\mathrm{sin}2x\mathrm{cos}x=\mathrm{sin}x}$$

Problem 1
Given below are two graphs that show the populations of foxes and rabbits in a national park over a 24 month period.
###IMAGE0###
###IMAGE1###
a. Explain why it is appropriate to model the number of rabbits and foxes as trigonometric functions of time.
b. Find an appropriate trigonometric function that models the number of rabbits,
$${r(t)}$$
, as a function of time, with
$$t$$
in months.
c. FInd an appropriate trigonometric function that models the number of foxes,
$$f(t)$$
, as a function of time, with
$$t$$
in months.
Problem 2
Have students participate in the Matching Graphs and Functions activity on page T-6 in
Representing Trigonometric Functions
from MARS.

Task
Use the unit circle and indicated triangle below to find the exact value of the sine and cosine of the special angle $\pi/4.$
###IMAGE0###

Problem 1
Evaluate:
$${\mathrm{tan}0^\circ}$$
$${\mathrm{cot}45^\circ}$$
$${\mathrm{csc}90^\circ}$$
Problem 2
Where is the value of
$${\mathrm{sec}\theta}$$
undefined?

Problem 1
FInd the value of the expression below:
$${\mathrm{sin}\frac{11\pi}{6}}$$
Problem 2
Explain why each of the following expressions are equivalent:
$${\mathrm{sin}\theta=\mathrm{sin}(x-\theta)}$$
$${\mathrm{cos}\theta=\mathrm{cos}(2\pi-\theta)}$$
$${\mathrm{cos}\theta=\mathrm{cos}(-\theta)}$$

Problem 1
Use the sum formula to determine a formula for finding the sine, cosine, and tangent of a double angle.
$${\mathrm{sin}(a+a)=}$$
$${\mathrm{cos}(a+a)=}$$
$${\mathrm{tan}(a+a)=}$$
Problem 2
Is the following statement true or false?
“If an angle is doubled, then the sine value of the angle is doubled.”
Write an equation that describes the statement.
Explain your reasoning algebraically.
Explain your reasoning using the graph shown.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 3
Prove the following identity:
$${{{\mathrm{cos}2x}\over{\mathrm{sin}x+\mathrm{cos}x}}=\mathrm{cos}x-\mathrm{sin}x}$$

What is the general solution for these equations?
$${-\mathrm{sin}x=\mathrm{cos}x}$$
$${\mathrm{sin}^2x+2\mathrm{cos}x-2=0}$$

Problem 1
Given that
$${f(x)={{\mathrm{cos}x+\mathrm{sin}x}\over\mathrm{cos}(2x)}}$$
$${g(x)={1\over{\mathrm{cos}x\mathrm{sin}x}}}$$
Answer the following questions:
Identify where both functions are undefines over the domain of
$${0 \leq x \leq 2\pi}$$
.
Show that, where defined,
$${f(x)= g(x)}$$
.
Problem 2
Fill in the blanks below using a number from
$$1$$
to
$$9$$
(no repeating), to find the equation whose solution is the largest value of
$$x$$
(from
$$0$$
to
$$360$$
, or
$$0$$
to
$${2\pi}$$
). What numbers will give you the smallest value of
$$x$$
?
###IMAGE0###

Problem 1
Given that
$${\mathrm{cos\theta}=\frac{\mathrm{adjacent}}{\mathrm{hypotenuse}}}$$
and
$${\mathrm{sin\theta}=\frac{\mathrm{opposite}}{\mathrm{hypotenuse}}}$$
, what is the definition of
$${\mathrm{tan\theta}}$$
in terms of sine and cosine?
Problem 2
What is the slope of the line containing
$${\overline{AD}}$$
?
###IMAGE0###
Show how you determine this using the trigonometric ratios for ANY angle.
Problem 3
Below is a diagram with a line segment tangent to the circle at point
$$E$$
.
$$\bigtriangleup ABC\approx \bigtriangleup ADE$$
. The radius of the circle is
$$1$$
.
###IMAGE1###
What is the value of
$$\overline{DE}$$
in terms of trigonometric ratios?

Problem 1
Below are six different trigonometric ratios, all noted on the unit circle.
“
$${\mathrm{sec}\theta}$$
” is “secant of theta”
“
$${\mathrm{cosec}\theta}$$
” is “cosecant of theta” (also can be written as
$${\mathrm{csc}\theta}$$
)
“
$${\mathrm{cot}\theta}$$
” is “cotangent of theta”
What is the relationship between the sine, cosine, and tangent and these three new trigonometric functions?
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Evaluate the following expressions:
a.
$${\mathrm{csc}(30^\circ)}$$
b.
$${\mathrm{cot}(135^\circ)}$$
c.
$${\frac{\mathrm{cos}(45^\circ)}{\mathrm{sec}(60^\circ)}}$$
d.
$${-\mathrm{cos}(-60^\circ)}$$
Problem 3
The image below shows where the values of sine and cosine are positive and negative. Fill out a similar image for cosecant, secant, tangent, and cotangent.
###IMAGE1###

Problem 1
Below is a graph of a sine function and a cosine function.
###IMAGE0###
Which of the following equations are true statements?
$${\mathrm{sin}(-\theta)=-\mathrm{sin}(\theta)}$$
$${\mathrm{sin}\left({\pi\over2}-\theta\right)=\mathrm{cos}(\theta)}$$
$${\mathrm{sin}(2\pi-\theta)=\mathrm{sin}(\theta)}$$
Problem 2
Verify the identity using definitions of functions.
$${{\mathrm{cot}(x)\over{\mathrm{csc}(x)}}=\mathrm{cos}(x)}$$

Problem 1
Show students the word doc linked in the blog post
Sinusoidal Sort
by Chris Hunter.
Ask, "Which functions, descriptions, and graphs match together?"
Problem 2
In each graph below, a function is graphed as a solid line and its reciprocal is graphed as a dotted line. What features of each function can you connect to its reciprocal?
###IMAGE0###
Based on what you notice, sketch graphs of
$${y=\mathrm{sec}x}$$
and
$${y=\mathrm{csc}x}$$
.

Task
In the picture below, the purple circle is the set of points in the plane whose distance from the origin (marked as $A$) is 1, often called the unit circle:
###IMAGE0###
Also pictured are some other points:  $B$ lies on the $x$-axis and the unit circle,  $C$ lies on the unit circle, and
$D$ is the midpoint of  $\overline{AB}$.  Angle $\angle BAC=60^\circ$.
Show that $\triangle ABC$ is equilateral and conclude that $\overleftrightarrow{CD}$ is perpendicular to $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$.
Use part (a) to find the values of $\sin{60^\circ}$ and $\cos{60^\circ}$.
Consider the following picture where $Q$ and $R$ are on the unit circle and $S$ is the intersection point of $\overleftrightarrow{QR}$ and the $x$-axis:
###IMAGE1###
Show that $\triangle PQR$ is equilateral and that $S$ is the midpoint of $\overline{QR}$.
Use  part (c) to find the values of $\sin{30^\circ}$ and $\cos{30^\circ}$.

Problem 1
What is the general solution for the equation
$${\mathrm{sin}x=\mathrm{csc}x}$$
?
Find the solution algebraically and graphically.
Problem 2
What is the general solution for
$${\mathrm{sin}^2x+\mathrm{cos}x+1=0}$$
?

Verify the identity.
$${{\mathrm{tan}(x)\over{\mathrm{sin}(x)}}=\mathrm{sec}(x)}$$
On what domain does this equation hold?

Problem 1
Sketch a graph of the sine function on the interval
$${0<x<2\pi}$$
showing all key points of the graph (vertical and horizontal intercepts and maximum and minimum points). Mark the coordinates of the maximum and minimum points and the intercepts.
Problem 2
Sketch a graph of the cosine function on the interval
$${0<x<2\pi}$$
showing all key points of the graph (vertical and horizontal intercepts and maximum and minimum points). Mark the coordinates of the maximum and minimum points and the intercepts.

Which one of the equations below matches the graph?
###IMAGE0###
A)
$${y=-3\mathrm{sin}(4x)}$$
B)
$${y=3\mathrm{cos}(4x)}$$
C)
$${y=3\mathrm{sin}\left(\frac{1}{4}x\right)}$$
D)
$${y=3\mathrm{cos}\left(\frac{1}{4}x\right)}$$

Problem 1
Solve each trigonometric equation such that
$${0\leq x\leq2\pi}$$
. Give answers in exact form.
$${2\mathrm{sin}(x)+\sqrt3=0}$$
Problem 2
Solve the trigonometric equation such that
$${0\leq x\leq2\pi}$$
. Round to three decimal places.
$${\sqrt5\mathrm{cos}(x)-2=0}$$

Solve the following equations for all values
$${0\leq x\leq2\pi}$$
.
$${3\mathrm{sin}\theta+2\mathrm{sin}^2\theta=-1}$$
$${\mathrm{sin}\theta\mathrm{cos}\theta-{1\over2}\mathrm{sin}\theta=0}$$

Tidal data for New Canal Station, located on the shore of Lake Pontchartrain, LA and Lake Charles, LA are shown below.
New Canal Station on Lake Pontchartrain, LA, Tide Chart
###TABLE0###
Lake Charles, LA, Tide Chart
###TABLE1###
a. Would a sinusoidal fucntion of the form
$${f(x)=A\mathrm{sin}(\omega (x-h))+k}$$
be appropriate to model the given data for each location? Explain your reasoning.
b. Write a sinusoidal function to model the data for New Canal Station.

khan_exercises.title: Trig values of special angles
khan_exercises.description: Find the sine and cosine of special angles, which are angles whose trig values we can determine without the use of a calculator.
topic_path: Trigonometric values on the unit circle

items.question_content:
**Find the following trigonometric values.**  
*Express your answers exactly.* 

$\cos(150^\circ)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]  

$\sin(150^\circ)=$ [[☃ expression 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

- Draw the desired angle on the unit circle and find a corresponding right triangle.

- Solve the right triangle using the known trigonometric values of the special angles $0^\circ$, $30^\circ$, $45^\circ$, or their complementary angles (as necessary). [[☃ explanation 1]]

- Determine the sign of the trigonometric values of the angle in question.

###Drawing $150^\circ$ and finding a corresponding right triangle

In the following drawing, $A$ is the intersection point of the unit circle with the line that forms an angle of $150^\circ$ with the positive $x$-axis. This means that the coordinates of $A$ are $\Large(\normalsize \cos(150^\circ),\sin(150^\circ)\Large)$.

If we draw a vertical line from $A$ to the $x$-axis, we obtain the right triangle $\triangle AOB$. We can use this triangle to find the coordinates of $A$.


[[☃ image 1]]

items.question_content:
**Find the following trigonometric values.**  
*Express your answers exactly.* 

$\cos(240^\circ)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]  

$\sin(240^\circ)=$ [[☃ expression 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

- Draw the desired angle on the unit circle and find a corresponding right triangle.

- Solve the right triangle using the known trigonometric values of the special angles $0^\circ$, $30^\circ$, $45^\circ$, or their complementary angles (as necessary). [[☃ explanation 1]]

- Determine the sign of the trigonometric values of the angle in question.

###Drawing $240^\circ$ and finding a corresponding right triangle

In the following drawing, $A$ is the intersection point of the unit circle with the line that forms an angle of $240^\circ$ with the positive $x$-axis. This means that the coordinates of $A$ are $\Large(\normalsize \cos(240^\circ),\sin(240^\circ)\Large)$.

If we draw a vertical line from $A$ to the $x$-axis, we obtain the right triangle $\triangle AOB$. We can use this triangle to find the coordinates of $A$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\cos(330^\circ)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]  

$\sin(330^\circ)=$ [[☃ expression 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

- Draw the desired angle on the unit circle and find a corresponding right triangle.

- Solve the right triangle using the known trigonometric values of the special angles $0^\circ$, $30^\circ$, $45^\circ$, or their complementary angles (as necessary). [[☃ explanation 1]]

- Determine the sign of the trigonometric values of the angle in question.

###Drawing $330^\circ$ and finding a corresponding right triangle

In the following drawing, $A$ is the intersection point of the unit circle with the line that forms an angle of $330^\circ$ with the positive $x$-axis. This means that the coordinates of $A$ are $\Large(\normalsize \cos(330^\circ),\sin(330^\circ)\Large)$.

If we draw a vertical line from $A$ to the $x$-axis, we obtain the right triangle $\triangle AOB$. We can use this triangle to find the coordinates of $A$.

items.question_content:
**Find the following trigonometric values.**  
*Express your answers exactly.* 

$\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]  

$\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)=$ [[☃ expression 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

- Draw the desired angle on the unit circle and find a corresponding right triangle.

- Solve the right triangle using the known trigonometric values of the special angles $0$, $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$, $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$, or their complementary angles (as necessary). [[☃ explanation 1]]

- Determine the sign of the trigonometric values of the angle in question.

###Drawing $\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)$ and finding a corresponding right triangle

In the following drawing, $A$ is the intersection point of the unit circle with the line that forms an angle of $\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)$ with the positive $x$-axis. This means that the coordinates of $A$ are $\Large(\normalsize \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right),\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)\Large)$.

If we draw a vertical line from $A$ to the $x$-axis, we obtain the right triangle $\triangle AOB$. We can use this triangle to find the coordinates of $A$.


[[☃ image 1]]

items.question_content:
**Find the following trigonometric values.**  
*Express your answers exactly.* 

$\cos\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]  

$\sin\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)=$ [[☃ expression 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

- Draw the desired angle on the unit circle and find a corresponding right triangle.

- Solve the right triangle using the known trigonometric values of the special angles $0$, $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$, $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$, or their complementary angles (as necessary). [[☃ explanation 1]]

- Determine the sign of the trigonometric values of the angle in question.

###Drawing $\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)$ and finding a corresponding right triangle

In the following drawing, $A$ is the intersection point of the unit circle with the line that forms an angle of $\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)$ with the positive $x$-axis. This means that the coordinates of $A$ are $\Large(\normalsize \cos\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3}\right),\sin\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)\Large)$.

If we draw a vertical line from $A$ to the $x$-axis, we obtain the right triangle $\triangle AOB$. We can use this triangle to find the coordinates of $A$.


[[☃ image 1]]

items.question_content:
**Find the following trigonometric values.**  
*Express your answers exactly.* 

$\cos\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{3}\right)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]  

$\sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{3}\right)=$ [[☃ expression 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

- Draw the desired angle on the unit circle and find a corresponding right triangle.

- Solve the right triangle using the known trigonometric values of the special angles $0$, $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$, $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$, or their complementary angles (as necessary). [[☃ explanation 1]]

- Determine the sign of the trigonometric values of the angle in question.

###Drawing $\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{3}\right)$ and finding a corresponding right triangle

In the following drawing, $A$ is the intersection point of the unit circle with the line that forms an angle of $\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{3}\right)$ with the positive $x$-axis. This means that the coordinates of $A$ are $\Large(\normalsize \cos\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{3}\right),\sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{3}\right)\Large)$.

If we draw a vertical line from $A$ to the $x$-axis, we obtain the right triangle $\triangle AOB$. We can use this triangle to find the coordinates of $A$.



[[☃ image 1]]

- Solve the right triangle using the known trigonometric values of the special angles $0^\circ$, $30^\circ$, $45^\circ$, or their complementary angles (as necessary). [[☃ explanation 1]]

- Determine the sign of the trigonometric values of the angle in question.

###Drawing $135^\circ$ and finding a corresponding right triangle

In the following drawing, $A$ is the intersection point of the unit circle with the line that forms an angle of $135^\circ$ with the positive $x$-axis. This means that the coordinates of $A$ are $\Large(\normalsize \cos(135^\circ),\sin(135^\circ)\Large)$.

If we draw a vertical line from $A$ to the $x$-axis, we obtain the right triangle $\triangle AOB$. We can use this triangle to find the coordinates of $A$.


[[☃ image 1]]
